segments.s038-g25: ابا is an archaic/poetic verb meaning “to refuse/scorn” used here without an explicit subject (narrative third person). The phrase تاج و با گنج links two items (tāj “crown” and ganj “treasure/wealth”) with و and the preposition با; نادیده rنج is an inverted adjective+noun phrase meaning “hardship unregarded” (literally “suffering not-seen/ignored”), so the whole segment says they scorned crowns and treasure and disregarded suffering.
segments.s038-g26: مگر here means “except.” زلفشان is “their lock(s) of hair.” دیده is the past-participial/nominal use (“seen,” i.e. ‘when seen’ or ‘to the sight’) rather than simply ‘eye,’ which makes this clause read as a conditional/qualifying exception. رنج شکنج is a nominal phrase ‘torment/pain that rends’ (رنج = suffering, شکنج = torment), so the segment means: except that, when their hair is seen, it is a torturing sight.
segments.s038-g27: بیاورد is a past narrative verb (“he/it brought”). هر three here functions as the distributive/collective modifier (هر سه = “all three” / “each of the three”). بدیشان is بد + ایشان contracted, a dative phrase meaning “to them,” and سپرد is another past verb “he entrusted/handed over.” The sequence places two past verbs in succession: ‘he brought (them) and handed them to them.’
segments.s038-g28: که introduces indirect speech/description (“that…”). سه ماه نو literally “three new moons” (ماه = moon), نو = “new/fresh,” and بود is the past copula “were.” و connects the next clause; سه شاه گرد reads “three kings (were) about/around” with گرد functioning adverbially/locatively (“about/around”), i.e. ‘three kings were gathered around.’ Altogether the clause reports the speaker’s description: they were like three new moons and three kings were assembled about them.
segments.s038-g29: ز (preposition) here has a poetic ablative/causal sense 'with/from' and introduces the cause ('with hatred'). به دل گفت is an idiom 'said to the heart' = 'said to himself'; گفت is past tense 'said' and the subject شاه یمن follows the verb (poetic word order: verb before subject).
segments.s038-g30: که introduces a reported/subordinate clause 'that…'. از marks source/agent ('from Afrīdūn'); بد آمد به من is an idiomatic intransitive expression literally 'bad came to me' meaning 'he (Afrīdūn) acted badly toward me' — آمد is simple past 'came' and به من is the dative 'to me'.
segments.s038-g31: بد از من is an exclamatory/woeful phrase 'bad for me' or 'woe unto me'. که introduces the content clause. هرگز 'never' modifies مبادم; مبادم is an archaic negative optative/subjunctive ('مبادم' = 'may I not be' / 'let me not be') and میان 'between' is the complement: 'may I never be between (them)'.
segments.s038-g32: که again gives the reason/explanation. ماده here = 'the female' (poetic/older usage for a woman or female lineage). شد is past 'became/was made', and از + تخم marks origin 'from the seed'; note the genitive chain 'تخم نره کیان' = 'the seed of the male Kiyān' (poetic omission of an explicit ezāfe is normal), so the clause states that the woman descended from the seed of the Kayan male.
segments.s038-g33: This clause begins with the prepositional phrase 'به اختر' (to/with regard to a star) followed by 'کس' in the sense 'no one' (negative indefinite). 'آن‌دان' is an adverb meaning 'so/that much' (i.e. 'such'), and the relative/causal 'که' introduces the defining content 'دخترش نیست' ('his daughter is not [there / does not exist]'). Word order is topical: the topic ('به اختر کس آن‌دان' — 'no one is so with regard to a star') comes before the relative clause 'که ...'.
segments.s038-g34: The conjunction/particle 'چو' here means 'when' (temporal); 'دختر بود' is a past-tense clause ('she was a daughter'). 'روشن' is an adjective 'bright' modifying 'اخترش' ('his/her star'), and the final 'نیست' negates that predicate: 'his/her star is not bright.' Note the verb 'بود' (was) is past while 'نیست' gives a present/state negation — a typical poetic juxtaposition indicating that even when she was a daughter, the star was not bright.
segments.s038-g35: 'به پیش' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of / before'; 'همه موبدان' is the indirect object/topic 'all the mōbeds (priests)'. 'سرو گفت' is a compact subject‑verb: 'the cypress said/spoke.' Word order places the location/audience ('before all the priests') up front for emphasis.
segments.s038-g36: The subordinating particle 'که' introduces reported content ('that ...'). 'زیبا بود' is the past copula 'was beautiful'. 'ماه را' (the moon + object marker 'را') is the topic/figure compared, and 'شاه جفت' is a nominative/nominal phrase meaning 'a king's match' or 'a mate/spouse (worthy of a king)'; together the clause reports that the moon's match (i.e. a peer/partner as beautiful as the moon) was beautiful. The object marker 'را' marks 'ماه' as the thing being paired/discussed.
segments.s038-g37: 'بدانید' is an imperative/declarative verb form from 'دانستن' here functioning as 'know/understand.' 'کین سه' is a classical phrasing meaning 'these three' (the demonstrative sense of 'کین' is archaic here — note the provided sense list did not include this, see senses). 'جهان‌بین خوی' is a compound: 'جهان‌بین' ('world-seer' or 'worldly-wise one') modified by the possessive/root 'خوی' with the following pronominal suffix; together the phrase points to the three persons being spoken of.
segments.s038-g38: 'سپردم' is the first-person past 'I entrusted/handed over.' 'بدیشان' is a dative ('to them'). 'بر آیین خویش' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'according to my custom/rites' (بر = according to/on, آیین = custom/rite, خویش + ش = 'my/one's own'). The clause therefore states the speaker's act of entrusting these three to those people under his own custom.
segments.s038-g39: This is a complex purposive clause: the prefix 'ب' + 'دان' together form 'بدان' ('know/consider'); 'تا' is used here in the sense 'so that/in order that'. 'چو' is 'when', 'دیده' 'the eye/sight', and 'بدارندشان' is a subjunctive/optative-like present ('they may hold/keep them'). Grammatically the clause reads 'Know this so that when they behold them, they may keep them (in view)'.
segments.s038-g40: This continuation repeats the temporal 'چو' ('when') and gives a parallel image: 'جان پیش دل' literally 'life before (the) heart' — i.e. hold life/beloved before one's heart. 'بر' here is the preposition 'on/upon', and 'نگارندشان' is a verbal form meaning 'they portray/depict/embrace them' (a poetic verb often meaning to paint or to keep lovingly). The clause conveys that when they cherish them deeply, they will represent/depict them.
segments.s038-g17: همه یال اسپان is a genitive phrase meaning “all the manes of the horses” (همه = “all/every”, یال = “mane”, اسپان = archaic plural “horses”). پر از + noun means “full of …”; here پر is an adjective “full (of)”, از introduces the contents, and می is the noun “wine” (not the present‑prefix).
segments.s038-g18: پراگنده + noun is an adjectival phrase “scattered …”; دینار is the noun “dinar/coin”. در زیر پی is a prepositional phrase “under/beneath the foot/hoof” (در = in/at, زیر = under, پی = “foot/track/hoof” here). Word order is poetic (adjective before noun and the prep‑phrase after).
segments.s038-g19: نشستن is the verbal noun “sitting” or purpose‑noun (i.e., a place for sitting); گهی is an adverb “sometimes”. ساخت is the past tense of ساختن “made/constructed”; شاه یمن is the subject following the verb in poetic order (“the king of Yemen”). So the clause means “the King of Yemen sometimes made (a place for) sitting.”
segments.s038-g20: همه نامداران is the subject “all the renowned/illustrious ones”; شدند is the past‑plural of شدن “became” or here “gathered/assembled”; انجمن is the predicate noun “assembly/meeting”. Poetic word order places the verb before its predicate noun.
segments.s038-g21: در گنجهای کهن is a locative prepositional phrase “in/within the ancient treasuries” (گنجهای = treasure‑houses, کهن = ancient). کرد is the past of کردن “did/made” used here with باز (again) — the verb appears after the prepositional phrase in poetic order: “opened again the ancient treasuries.”
segments.s038-g22: گشاد is used as a past/participial sense ‘opened’ or ‘laid open’; آنچه is the relative “that which/whatever”; یک چند گه is a temporal phrase “for a short while / for a few moments” (یک چند = a little/a few, گه = time/occasion). بود is past “was” and راز is the predicate noun “secret.” The clause says he opened what for a short time had been secret.
segments.s038-g23: سه خورشید رخ = “three sun(s) of (their) face(s)” (a poetic way to say three sun‑bright faces). را is the object/definite marker attached to رخ. چو is the simile “like/as”, and باغ بهشت is “a garden of paradise” (باغ = garden, بهشت = paradise). The phrase compares the faces to a paradise garden.
segments.s038-g24: که introduces the explanatory/subordinate clause “for/that …”; موبد is the subject “mōbed” (Zoroastrian priest). چو ایشان = “like them”. صنوبر is “pine (tree)”. نکشت here is classical past (with ن‑) meaning “did not plant” (i.e., no priest planted a pine like them); the negative prefix + past verb is typical in older Persian poetry.
segments.s038-g1: سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward' and governs the noun خانه ('house'); رفتند is a 3rd-person plural past tense verb 'they went/they hurried'. هر سه ('all three') emphasizes the plural subject and follows the verb for poetic word order. چوباد is an adverbial simile 'like the wind/quickly' modifying the action رفتند.
segments.s038-g2: شب آمد is a simple temporal clause 'night came' introducing the time. بخفتند is the past 3rd-person plural of خفتن 'they slept' (the initial b- is the classical poetic form). پیروز و شاد are predicate adjectives after the verb describing the subjects: 'victorious and happy', coordinated by و.
segments.s038-g3: چو here means 'when' introducing a temporal clause. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'; زد is the 3rd-person past of زدن 'to strike/to cast' and combines with عکس ('عکس زدن') to mean 'to cast/throw a reflection'. برآسمان is a prepositional phrase 'upon the sky'.
segments.s038-g4: پراگند is an adjectival/verb-like form meaning 'scattered' or 'were scattered' (poetic economy often omits an explicit auxiliary). بر marks the area 'over/upon', and لاژورد ارغوان are two color-nouns ('lapis‑blue' and 'crimson') placed together to describe what was scattered across the sky.
segments.s038-g5: برفتند is a past 3rd-person plural verb 'they departed/they went off' (بر- here is a classical prefix used in motion verbs). و links clauses. هر سه 'all three' again emphasizes the plural; بیاراستند is past 3rd-person plural of بیاراستن 'they adorned/dressed (themselves)', with the bi- prefix forming the transitive/causative-like classical verb.
segments.s038-g6: موبدان خواستند is a straightforward past clause 'they requested/summoned mobeds (Zoroastrian priests)' with خواستند as 3rd-person plural past of خواستن. خویشتن is an emphatic reflexive 'for themselves' or 'their own'. ابا here is an older/poetic particle that in this context conveys absence of reluctance ('without hesitation' / not being unwilling) before the action of requesting the priests.
segments.s038-g7: کشیدند is the past 3rd-person plural of کشیدن; in military/poetic contexts it means 'they moved forward / marched' or 'they drew up'. با لشکری is an adjunct 'with an army' (با = 'with', لشکری = 'troops/army'). چون سپهر is a simile 'like the sky/heaven', qualifying the size or splendour of the army.
segments.s038-g8: همه is 'all/every', نامداران is the plural noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones', and خورشیدچهر is a compound epithet 'sun‑faced' placed after the noun to describe those named (poetic, attributive epithet). Together: 'all the famed, sun‑faced ones.'
segments.s038-g41: خروشید is a 3rd-person past singular verb meaning “it roared” (classical/poetic past). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” بار here means “burden/load” and غریبان is the plural “of the strangers/exiles” (genitive relationship understood in the phrase); ببست is the simple past “(he/it) bound/tied.” The clause literally reads “it roared and bound the strangers’ load,” with the verb ببست governing بار غریبان.
segments.s038-g42: This is a nominal phrase giving an image: ابر “the cloud” + پشت “on the back” (pushed together as a locative/position: ‘on the back of’) + شرزه ‘rebellious/insolent’ modifying هیونان “beasts/monsters.” مست is a predicative adjective “drunk/intoxicated” (used figuratively). Word order (noun + modifier + adjective) is typical of Persian poetic lines: “the cloud, drunken, on the backs of the rebellious beasts.”
segments.s038-g43: ز is the poetic preposition “from / because of,” here giving cause (“because of/from the ...”). گوهر is “jewel/gem.” یمن is used as an attributive of گوهر (the gem of Yaman/Yemen); گشت is the past of شدن “became” (here “was / became”), and افروخته is an adjective/participle “kindled, ablaze.” The clause reads literally “from the Yemeni jewel it became kindled.”
segments.s038-g44: عماری is a noun meaning a dwelling/edifice (an architectural thing). یک is the numeral “one.” اندردگر is an older/poetic compound meaning “in another place/otherwise” (here functioning adverbially), and دوخته is a past participle “sewn/stitched.” The phrase literally describes “one dwelling sewn into/within another” (i.e. an edifice joined or set within another).
segments.s038-g45: چو here means “when.” فرزند را places فرزند with the topical/definite marker را for emphasis (classical Persian allows را with a topicalized subject). باشد is the classical present/subjunctive form of بودن used here to mean “has / is endowed with” (literary present). آئین “custom/manners” and فر “farr/royal glory” are the two qualities predicated of the child.
segments.s038-g46: گرامی is an adjective “honored/dear.” به دل بر is a short phrase meaning “to/into the heart” (به + دل, with بر adding a sense of ‘on/upon’ the feeling). The correlative چه ... چه ... functions as “whether ... or ...” (introducing the alternatives ماده “female” and نر “male”); the clause means “he is cherished in the heart, whether female or male.”
segments.s038-g47: به سوی is the directional phrase “toward.” فریدون is the proper name (Fereydun). نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن “they set/placed” and here combines with روی “face” to mean “they turned/set their faces (toward).” In classical Persian نهادن + روی is a common paratactic way to say “turned their faces to...”
segments.s038-g48: جوانان is the plural “young men / youths.” بینادل is a rare or uncertain reading in the manuscript tradition (the lexicon entry flags it as an uncertain/rare word); grammatically it behaves as an adjective/adverb modifying the youths (see note). راه جوی is a compound: راه “path/road” + جوی (from جستن) “seeking/searching,” so راه جوی means “seeking the road” (i.e. they went in search of the way).
segments.s038-g9: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' (not 'as' or 'if') introducing a subordinate time clause. از آمدنشان is a noun phrase ('of their coming') with از meaning 'of/concerning'; شد آگاه is a compound where شد (past of شدن) = 'became' and آگاه = 'aware'. سرو is the noun 'the cypress' and is the logical subject that 'became aware' in this poetic, slightly inverted order.
segments.s038-g10: This clause uses a poetic inversion: the verb/adjective بیاراست ('adorned' / 'did adorn') precedes the nominal subject لشکر ('the host/army'). چو is the comparative 'as/like', and پر تذرو is a noun phrase where پر functions as 'full (of)' and تذرو (here used as a noun derived from a verbal root) conveys the sense of scattering/fluttering — together 'full of fluttering plumes'. The object of adornment (the cypress) is implicit, carried over from the previous clause.
segments.s038-g11: فرستادشان is the past verb 'sent' with the clitic شان ('them/his') attached; in this poetic order the verb phrase appears before the nominative-like phrase لشکری گشن پیش, read as 'an army, hungry, sent before (them)'. پیش functions as 'before/ahead'. The overall sense is 'he sent forward a hungry army.'
segments.s038-g12: The correlative construction چه ... و چه ... marks alternatives ('whether ... or ...'). Here it enumerates groups composing or accompanying the force: بیگانه 'foreign/strange', فرزانگان 'the learned/sages', و links them, and چه خوی ش = 'or his own kin' where خوی + the enclitic ش = 'his kin'. This clause therefore means 'whether foreigners, learned men, or his own (people)'.
segments.s038-g13: شدند is the past plural of شدن 'they became / they were' and appears before the demonstrative این ('these/this') in a poetic, somewhat inverted order. پرمایه is an adjective/noun meaning 'substantial/wealthy' describing 'these three', and اندر یمن is a locative phrase 'in Yemen' (یمن here is the place-name). The clause thus states that these three valuable entities were located in Yemen.
segments.s038-g14: برون آمدند is a compound verbal phrase 'came out / came forth' (برون = 'out/forth' + آمدند = 'they came') and is followed by the source phrase از یمن 'from Yemen'. The grammatical subject 'مرد و زن' (men and women) follows the verb, a common poetic ordering: 'men and women came out of Yemen.'
segments.s038-g15: همی is a frequent Shahnameh particle indicating continued or habitual action ('still/continually'). گوهر و زعفران are direct objects 'gems and saffron', and ریختند is the past plural of ریختن 'they poured / scattered / cast (out)'. The clause means 'they poured out gems and saffron.'
segments.s038-g16: Again همی marks ongoing action. مشک با می‌برآمیختند is a verb phrase 'they mixed musk with wine' (با = 'with'; می‌برآمیختند = imperfective/past continuous 'they were mingling / mixing'). The word order places the objects before the verb, typical of this poetic style.

sentences.s038-s13: They scorned crowns and treasure and paid no heed to hardship — except that, when their tresses were seen, they were a torturing pain to the eye.
sentences.s038-s14: He brought all three and handed them over to them, saying that they were three new moons and three kings were gathered about.
sentences.s038-s15: Burning with hatred in his heart, the king of Yemen said to himself that Afrīdūn had treated him ill.
sentences.s038-s16: Woe to me — may I never come between them — for the female was born from the seed of the Kayan male.
sentences.s038-s17: No one's star is such when his child is a daughter; when she was a daughter, her star was not bright.
sentences.s038-s18: Before all the mōbeds the cypress spoke, saying that the match for the moon was beautiful.
sentences.s038-s19: Know that I have entrusted these three world-seers to them according to my own custom.
sentences.s038-s20: Know this so that, when they hold them in sight, when they set life before the heart, they will portray them.
sentences.s038-s9: All the horses' manes were full of musk and wine, and dinars were scattered beneath their hooves.
sentences.s038-s10: At times the king of Yemen made (a place for) sitting, and all the renowned men gathered together.
sentences.s038-s11: He opened again the ancient treasuries; he opened up what for a little while had been a secret.
sentences.s038-s12: Their three sunlike faces were like a garden of paradise, for no mobed planted a pine like them.
sentences.s038-s1: All three hurried toward the house like the wind; night came and they slept, victorious and joyful.
sentences.s038-s2: When the sun cast its reflection upon the sky, azure and crimson were scattered across it.
sentences.s038-s3: They departed, and all three adorned themselves; without hesitation they asked for mobeds for their own selves.
sentences.s038-s4: They advanced with an army as vast as the heavens — all the renowned, sun‑faced heroes.
sentences.s038-s21: It roared and bound the strangers' load; the cloud, drunken, upon the backs of the rebellious beasts.
sentences.s038-s22: From the Yemeni jewel it was kindled; one dwelling was stitched into another.
sentences.s038-s23: When a child has custom and farr, he is dear to the heart — whether female or male.
sentences.s038-s24: They turned their faces toward Fereydun; the young men (بینادل — uncertain reading) sought the road.
sentences.s038-s5: When the cypress became aware of their coming, the host adorned (it) as if full of fluttering plumes.
sentences.s038-s6: He sent ahead of them a hungry host — whether foreign or local, the learned and his own kin alike.
sentences.s038-s7: These three valuable ones were in Yemen; men and women came forth from Yemen.
sentences.s038-s8: They poured out gems and saffron; they mingled musk with wine.
